clinical_coherence.notes: 25F Caucasian with migraine, anxiety, and subtle neurological symptoms (episodic vertigo, transient sensory changes, fatigue). The normal neurological exam with only subtle findings (questionable nystagmus) is consistent with a diagnostic puzzle where objective signs are minimal. Prior vertigo attributed to vestibular neuritis, prior tingling attributed to migraine aura -- both realistic misattributions. Comorbid migraine with topiramate use adds genuine diagnostic confusion.

tool_output_consistency.notes: Initial MRI is equivocal with non-specific white matter lesions and low confidence (0.55). CSF is normal including absent OCBs. VEP normal. Initial labs unremarkable except low-titer ANA and low vitamin D. Follow-up MRI at 3 months reveals new enhancing Dawson finger lesion and juxtacortical lesion, now meeting McDonald criteria. This progression is perfectly calibrated for a diagnostic puzzle.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G35 correct. The differential appropriately leads with migraine-related WMLs as the main competing diagnosis. Critical actions correctly emphasize serial imaging when initial findings are inconclusive. Key reasoning about anchoring bias from pre-existing migraine is excellent.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Outstanding diagnostic puzzle: normal exam, equivocal initial MRI overlapping with migraine WMLs, normal CSF (OCB-negative MS occurs in 5-10%), normal VEP, normal spine MRI. Diagnosis only becomes clear on follow-up imaging. The complexity genuinely challenges diagnostic reasoning.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 25F Caucasian with migraine and anxiety is highly realistic. These comorbidities are common in young women and create genuine diagnostic confusion with early MS.

educational_value.notes: Outstanding teaching case addressing anchoring bias, migraine vs MS white matter lesions, OCB-negative MS, the importance of periventricular lesion morphology, and serial MRI strategy. One of the best puzzle cases in the set.

strengths: Perfectly calibrated diagnostic puzzle with genuine ambiguity on initial presentation
Normal CSF including absent OCBs reflects real-world OCB-negative MS (5-10% of cases)
Migraine comorbidity creates authentic diagnostic confusion
Follow-up MRI evolution demonstrating McDonald criteria fulfillment over time
Teaching points about anchoring bias are highly relevant clinically
